reviews.review_msg: This course is a tough nut to crack and can get frustrating at times. If you put effort into you will feel accomplished tho it's hard to understand. TAs are pretty helpful it's just simply a boring class tbh and you need to participate in tutorials. Exam was nerve wrecking so go to Simon before the final

I am delighted to have ended this course w a good mark however material itself was not easy. TAs were slow when it came to marking and though we had 3 weeks to complete each assignment, you cant count on TAs to also get back to you within the time frame, it was at least 1.5 month wait for a mark. Simon was accommodating and pretty approachable tho.

Lectures were engaging. The final was fair and reflected the lectures and assignments. There are 3 assignments worth 15%, 15%, and 20% respectively which take a very long time to complete (30-50+ hr), but we are given 3 weeks to complete each one. Work on them everyday for a few hours. Little feedback is given back. Self-teach through the textbook.

Simon has engaging lectures and makes otherwise boring topics fun to learn about. What students are expected to know for assignments and examinations is made very clear.

As other reviews mentioned, he does go into tangents about niche topics, but they're always quite informative, so I wouldn't necessarily list this as a bad quality of his.

No feedback whatsoever on assignments and slow responding to emails. Ta's try to explain the criteria in tutorials but it ultimately dosen't matter as feedback on the previous assignment is given when the next one was submitted, thus you have no real chance of improving the way you do them. And feedback itself is generic and not directed towards u

I'm a bit torn on Simon. On one hand he's a terrible grader: overly critical with the lack of rubric making it impossible to argue for marks. However, he's by far the most engaging professor I've ever had, with incredibly nuanced takes, a passionate attitude, and an open discussion format that helped me form a deep understanding.

Dr. Pollon will (guaranteed) go on tangents and get really passionate about topics, unrelated or not. He will manage to bring the class back to the topic at hand and will make sure to only test you on what was covered. In the exams we got prompts for topics that have been discussed in class. The criteria is always vague for writing assignments.

Simon is a fun lecturer but he's old school. No notes provided, lectures are off the cuff and written on the blackboard. He is great at engaging with his students and does not shy away from sharing his beliefs or taking shots at those beliefs he doesn't agree with. Exams and assignments are hard. Must attend every lecture. Tough, enjoyable course.

Good dude
Done all his courses
He cancelled so many classes. His lectures are heavy and you need to make notes of everything.

Simon does his best but the lack of slides/extra resources makes it feel like he wings the lectures and causes lectures to be all over the place. i left most lectures feeling like i just sat through 3 hours of the missile knows where it is meme. opaque grading, esp. participation, feels like is chosen to curve the grades more than anything

Simon sometimes goes on tangents but overall the lectures are enjoyable. The main issue is that he is very chill and receptive to students' ideas in class, but then marks papers and assignments super harshly. He might be receptive to a comment you make in class, but then he will be unnecessarily critical of the same idea in written form.
He is an okay prof at best. He can be a bit of a tough grader because he searches for very specific details. If you don't know much about philosophy, you may struggle!

Nice guy. This was a good class to take online, I like the way things were laid out. Simon was super compassionate to my class, it seemed like everyone was struggling a bit this semester with life stuff. I would take another class with Dr Pollon, he was a tough but fair grader. Not hard to pass this class, but requires effort to get a good grade

interesting lectures, attendance is only mandatory to get participation, but content is not all relevant. gives good feedback on writing pieces and just overall a really chill guy. I enjoyed his class

Professor Pollon is an awesome philosophy prof, he's incredibly knowledgeable inside and out of the field so even if he goes off on tangents it's still interesting. Bit of a tough marker on reflections, but he's generally flexible on what topic you choose to talk about. Participation in class will definitely put you ahead of the others so chime in.

So Simon was my first philosophy teacher and I loved it. His lectures are funny with occasional swear words, sometimes off topic, but useful for books you read in class. The SWA's weren't too bad, just need to read a lot to do well. Essays were fairly easy with feedback he gives, and you pick 5 out of 10 questions based on the books for the final

Simon is one rad dude. His lectures are great, sometimes off topic but he always brings it back in a relevant way. His lectures really help to clarify the readings, and he's super approachable. He's also incredibly fair in his testing! If he hasn't covered smth in lecture he wont test you on it. Overall a great prof, I really recommend him
Very engaging courses, would take again. Each class was looked forward to, and enjoyed all the debates that ensued.

Simon is a good prof BUT he is super unorganized and likes to go off on tangents a lot. He doesn't make any lecture slides so it's often hard to go back and study. Lucky the class is fairly easy and readings are short

Philosophy is hard to read. Ancient Philosophy is even harder to read. His lectures make up for it. He explains things clearly and goes off on interesting tangents. He teaches in a style similar to ancient philosophers. However this means you need to go to lecture, as he makes no slides. Go to class, maybe make notes in class, and you'll be fine.
